summary: Charity Majors explains that startup hiring rejections are almost never about a candidate being 'not good enough,' but rather about constrained slots, team composition needs, and fit alignment. She walks through eleven honest reasons candidates don't get hired at Honeycomb, emphasizing that most are about organizational constraints and team dynamics rather than individual merit.
key_points: Startup hiring is defined by scarcity — few slots tied to critical company goals, not an open door for anyone who passes a bar
Teams need diversity of level, background, and skills — not just the most senior or credentialed people
Communication skills are foundational to high-performing teams and are weighted heavily in hiring
Being too senior can be just as much a reason for rejection as not being senior enough
The core hiring question isn't 'how good are they?' but 'what will they need to be successful and can we provide it?'
money_quotes: People stay at shitty jobs far, far longer than they ought to, just because interviews can be so genuinely crushing to your spirit and sense of self.
A team staffed with nothing but extremely senior developers will be a dysfunctional, bored and contentious team where no one is really growing up or being challenged as they should.
We believe that if you can explain what you did and why, you can definitely do the work, and the reverse is not necessarily true.

If we brought you in for an interview, we already think you're awesome. Period. Now we're just trying to figure out if you narrowly intersect the skill sets we are lacking that we need to succeed this year.
The phrase you would hear over and over is not 'how good are they?', but 'what will they need to be successful? can we provide the support they need?'
key_insight: Startup hiring rejections are almost always about constrained organizational needs and team composition rather than a judgment of individual worth, and the real question hiring managers ask is whether they can set a candidate up for success — not whether the candidate is 'good enough.'
